headline: Bybit is a centralized exchange; all protocol rules, custody, and funds are controlled by a single private company with no on-chain governance, no timelock, and no verifiable upgrade mechanism

findings.text: Bybit is a CEX (Bybit Fintech Limited, incorporated in the British Virgin Islands). There are no publicly documented smart contract 'owner' or 'admin' roles for the core exchange. User funds are held in company-controlled hot and cold wallets. The Bybit Proof-of-Reserves page lists exchange-held addresses on Ethereum and other chains (e.g. Ethereum address 0x77134cbc06cb00b66f4c7e623d5fdbf6777635ec is a known Bybit cold wallet listed on their PoR page), but these are simple custody wallets, not governed smart contracts. There is no pendingAdmin, governor, or proxy admin to read.

No smart contract upgrade mechanism exists for Bybit's core exchange. The exchange operates as a traditional Web2/CeFi platform. User balances and matching engine state are maintained in Bybit's centralized databases, not in upgradeable on-chain contracts. Bybit does operate some on-chain programs (e.g. for Bybit Chain / Mantle integrations and for its native staking and yield products), but the core exchange custody and trading rules are not governed by any on-chain proxy or upgrade mechanism accessible to users.

Execution path for rule changes: there is no on-chain execution path. All changes to trading rules, fee schedules, withdrawal policies, listing/delisting of assets, and custody arrangements are made unilaterally by Bybit Fintech Limited as a company, with no on-chain timelock, no queuing period, and no public proposal-and-vote mechanism. The company publishes announcements via its website/blog, but these are informational only and impose no binding delay. The uncontested fast path delay is 0 seconds.

No on-chain multisig controls the core exchange rules. Bybit's publicly known Ethereum wallet addresses (e.g. 0x77134cbc06cb00b66f4c7e623d5fdbf6777635ec and associated wallets listed on Bybit's PoR attestation) are plain EOA-style or simple custody addresses. There is no published multisig arrangement for exchange rule governance. After Bybit suffered a $1.5B exploit in February 2025 (the largest exchange hack in history, traced to a compromised Safe{Wallet} frontend targeting Bybit's ETH cold wallet multisig), it was revealed that Bybit used a Gnosis Safe multisig for its ETH cold wallet — but this is a custody arrangement for holding ETH reserves, not a governance multisig for protocol rules. The signer threshold/composition for that custody multisig was not publicly disclosed prior to the hack.

No on-chain governance exists. There is no Governor contract, no GovernorBravo, no OZ Governor, no voting token with governance rights over the exchange's core operations. The BIT token (and its successor, the MNT token on Mantle) has governance over the Mantle ecosystem but not over Bybit's CEX rules or custody.

No formal on-chain emergency pause mechanism. Bybit can and does suspend withdrawals or trading unilaterally as a company decision (e.g. during the February 2025 hack, Bybit suspended ETH withdrawals for several hours). There is no on-chain guardian role with a defined time cap — the company can freeze user access to funds with immediate effect and indefinitely, subject only to regulatory and reputational constraints.

Power tier: T1. Bybit Fintech Limited, as a single centralized entity, can (and has demonstrated the ability to): pause withdrawals indefinitely (T1 — pause withdrawals), change fee parameters without bound (T1 — no on-chain enforcement), delist assets, change custody arrangements, and in the extreme case (insolvency, hack, regulatory seizure) could result in total loss of user funds. The February 2025 hack demonstrated that a single compromised signing ceremony could drain $1.5B in ETH from a custody multisig. The blast radius is maximal: the company controls all user funds with no on-chain constraints on its behavior.

steelman.red: Bybit is a single private company that unilaterally controls all user funds, trading rules, and withdrawal access with zero on-chain timelock, no governance, and no verifiable multisig threshold — the February 2025 $1.5B hack proved the custody arrangement could be compromised and withdrawals suspended with no recourse, making this the textbook T1-no-timelock-single-entity scenario.
steelman.orange: Bybit has survived since 2018, publishes Proof-of-Reserves attestations, uses cold storage for the majority of funds, and is regulated in multiple jurisdictions — while centralized, its operational track record and reserve transparency are better than many CEXes, arguably placing it in a 'centralized but credibly managed' tier rather than pure red.
steelman.green: A CEX operating under regulatory oversight in multiple jurisdictions with published PoR attestations, industry-standard custodial security practices, and a large audited reserve could be considered green on control if one accepts that legal/regulatory frameworks substitute for on-chain governance constraints.

rationale.verdict: Choosing red because Bybit Fintech Limited holds T1 power — full unilateral control over all user funds, trading rules, and withdrawal access — with zero on-chain timelock, no verifiable multisig governance structure, and no on-chain constraints of any kind. The February 2025 exploit (publicly reported at ~$1.5B in ETH drained from Bybit's cold wallet multisig) directly demonstrated that the custody arrangement can be compromised in a single signing event with no on-chain delay or warning period for users. The uncontested fast-path delay is 0 seconds. No legal or reputational argument qualifies as a substitute for on-chain enforcement under this rubric's T1/red criteria.

evidence.shows: Bybit's Proof-of-Reserves page listing exchange-controlled wallet addresses across multiple chains; confirms CEX custody model with company-controlled wallets, not smart contract governance
Bybit labeled Ethereum cold wallet address — a plain custody address, not a governed smart contract with an admin or upgrade mechanism; no proxy pattern, no owner() function

Bybit's official statement on the February 2025 security breach; confirms that Bybit's ETH cold wallet (a Gnosis Safe multisig) was drained of approximately $1.5B in ETH via a compromised signing UI; confirms unilateral suspension of ETH withdrawals by the company with no on-chain delay

Bybit exploited Safe multisig address (labeled 'Bybit: Hacked' on Etherscan) — confirms custody was via a Gnosis Safe multisig with no publicly disclosed threshold or signer composition prior to the incident; the contract is a standard Safe proxy with no timelock or governance overlay

Bybit announcement channel — all exchange rule changes (fees, listings, policies) are made via unilateral company announcements with no on-chain queuing or timelock; confirms absence of decentralized governance

Bybit Terms of Service — confirms users transfer custody of assets to Bybit Fintech Limited; Bybit can suspend accounts, freeze withdrawals, and modify terms unilaterally; no on-chain governance constraint is mentioned

unknowns: C1: No on-chain Read Contract tab exists for Bybit's core exchange — it is a CEX with no governing smart contracts; cannot read owner/admin/pendingAdmin because no such contracts exist in the DeFi sense
C3: Exact internal Bybit governance process (who must approve rule changes, what internal quorum or approval chain exists) is not publicly disclosed; only the outcome (announcements) is observable

C4: Signer composition and threshold of Bybit's custody multisigs (including the hacked Safe) were not publicly disclosed; the February 2025 hack revealed a Safe multisig was used but its m-of-n configuration and signer identities remain undisclosed

C5: No on-chain governance contract exists for the CEX; BIT/MNT governance applies only to the Mantle L2 ecosystem, not to Bybit exchange rules — full scope of MNT governance over any Bybit CEX parameters not verified
C6: No on-chain emergency pause contract with defined parameters; the company's internal escalation procedures for halting trading/withdrawals are not publicly specified with time bounds

admin_addresses.role: Bybit ETH cold wallet (custody, not governance)
Bybit hacked Safe multisig (former ETH cold custody wallet, drained Feb 2025)

protocol_metadata.about: Bybit is one of the world's largest centralized cryptocurrency exchanges, founded in 2018 and headquartered in Dubai, serving over 50 million registered users for spot trading, perpetual and futures contracts, options, and earn products. Users deposit assets into Bybit's custody (held in company-controlled hot and cold wallets) and trade on a centralized order book; assets are not held in user-controlled smart contracts. Bybit was subject to the largest exchange hack in history in February 2025, when approximately $1.5 billion in ETH was stolen from a Gnosis Safe cold wallet via a compromised signing interface. Bybit operates the Mantle L2 blockchain ecosystem through its BitDAO successor entity.
